subjectLine: Blood panel predicts Alzheimer's a decade out 🧠
borisTake: The complement paper is the one to flag this week — a blood test that distinguishes future AD converters from normal agers a full decade before symptoms, using proteins most practitioners have never ordered. This belongs in comprehensive longevity bloodwork immediately, sitting alongside IL-6, CRP, and the standard inflammatory panel.
leadAnalysis: A 10-year longitudinal cohort of 235 initially cognitively normal adults found that five plasma complement proteins — C4, C4b, Factor I, Factor D, and Properdin — deviated progressively from normative aging trajectories exclusively in individuals who later converted to Alzheimer's disease. These deviations correlated robustly with established CSF biomarkers of amyloid and tau pathology, confirming this is AD-specific remodelling rather than generic inflammaging. The mechanism points to aberrant innate immune activation in the brain's maintenance circuitry: complement drives microglial behaviour at synapses, and dysregulation here likely accelerates synapse pruning long before plaques become visible on imaging. Clinically, this five-protein panel — cheap, peripheral, and requiring only standard plasma — could stratify dementia risk far earlier than any current diagnostic tool.
oneNumber: 57.2% | Reduction in atherosclerotic lesion area when macrophage-specific SEMA7A was knocked out in mice — and a pharmacological integrin β1 inhibitor recapitulates the effect

papers.title: Systemic Complement Factors in Aging, Alzheimer's Disease and Other Dementias: A Longitudinal Study Over 10 Years
papers.summary: In 235 cognitively normal individuals followed for 10 years, five complement proteins (C4, C4b, Factor I, Factor D, Properdin) showed progressive deviation from normative aging trajectories exclusively in AD converters. A parallel cross-sectional cohort of 323 participants confirmed the findings across dementia subtypes. Peripheral complement remodelling correlates with CSF amyloid/tau and constitutes a distinct pre-AD blood biomarker panel.

papers.title: Transient Hepatic Reconstitution of Trophic Factors Enhances Aged Immunity
papers.summary: A single transient intervention restoring youthful hepatic trophic factor secretion — without long-term genetic modification — rejuvenated thymic output and naive T cell numbers in aged mice. The liver emerges as a systemic orchestrator of immune aging, and the effect was durable across several months. Opens a pharmacological window for periodic liver-targeted interventions to reset immune age.

papers.title: Macrophage SEMA7A Drives Atherosclerosis via Integrin β1/JNK/MSR1 Axis
papers.summary: Macrophage-specific Sema7a deletion reduced atherosclerotic lesion area by 57.2% in a mouse model and improved plaque stability. SEMA7A signals via integrin β1 → JNK → MSR1 to promote foam cell formation and lipid uptake. Pharmacological inhibition with integrin receptor antagonist GLPG0187 reproduced the benefit, identifying a tractable new drug target upstream of cholesterol-driven plaque progression.

papers.title: MRI-Based Multi-Organ Biological Age Clocks for Healthy Aging and Disease Assessment
papers.summary: The MULTI Consortium used imaging data to build organ-specific biological age clocks for brain, heart, liver, kidney, and lung from routine MRI scans. Each organ's biological age diverges from chronological age in disease-specific patterns, and gaps between organ clocks predicted mortality and disease onset. Non-invasive, accessible, and applicable to existing clinical imaging data.

papers.title: NAD⁺ Supplementation for Anti-Aging and Wellness: A PRISMA-Guided Systematic Review
papers.summary: Comprehensive PRISMA review synthesises preclinical and clinical evidence for NMN and NR across multiple endpoints. Preclinical data remains consistently strong for mitochondrial function, NAD+ repletion, and healthspan extension. Clinical evidence shows meaningful improvements in muscle strength and endurance in older adults; metabolic benefits are cleaner in insulin-resistant populations. Dose, timing, and bioavailability remain under-standardised.

papers.title: From Cell Reprogramming to Tissue Rejuvenation: Countering Aging by Targeting a Gerozyme
papers.source: PubMed / Annual Review of Pharmacology and Toxicology
papers.summary: Helen Blau's lab reviews the Gerozyme concept — a class of enzymes that accumulate with age and drive cellular dysfunction. Inhibiting gerozymes in aged muscle stem cells restores youthful regenerative capacity in mice. The review maps the mechanistic overlap between gerozyme inhibition, partial reprogramming, and senolytic clearance, framing these as complementary rather than competing strategies.

papers.title: Electric Field EHD Bioprinting Produces Aligned Skeletal Muscle That Restores Function In Vivo
papers.source: Fight Aging! / Smart Materials in Manufacturing
papers.summary: Electrohydrodynamic (EHD) bioprinting uses applied voltage to align fibrin nanofibers during printing, producing skeletal muscle constructs with native-like cellular architecture. Addition of conductive polymers promotes myotube maturation. In a rat muscle defect model, EHD-printed constructs integrated successfully and restored lost function at the injury site — a key proof-of-concept for vascularisation-independent regeneration.
